title: The Width-Volume Inequality
overall_summary: The paper defines k-width of a Riemannian manifold or Euclidean domain using sweepouts by flat relative k-cycles, and proves the Euclidean width-volume inequality W_k(U) â‰¤ C(n) Vol(U)^{k/n} for bounded open U âŠ‚ R^n. The core construction is to bend a family of parallel k-planes around a translated lattice k-skeleton, so that each sweeping cycle lies mostly in the skeleton and has controlled mass. The paper refines this to subsets of rectangles and packages the result as packing-width estimates, then uses these estimates to prove lower bounds for k-dilation of nonzero-degree maps between rectangles. It also proves that the optimal (nâˆ’1)-dilation between two n-rectangles is given up to dimensional constants by an explicit maximum of monomials in the side-length ratios, with snake maps giving matching upper bounds. For the original problem, the most directly useful ingredients are the monotonicity of k-width under degree maps with bounded k-dilation, the rectangle-adapted width-volume and packing-width estimates, the lower bounds for D_k(R,S), and the singular-value fact that a map with small 2-dilation also has controlled 3-dilation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 1 monotonicity of k-width under restriction and degree maps
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let W_k(M) denote the k-width of a compact oriented Riemannian n-manifold M, with relative flat k-cycles used when M has boundary; W_k(M) is the infimum over all sweepout families of k-cycles of the supremal k-mass in the family. If M is an open subset of a Riemannian manifold N with the induced metric, then W_k(M) â‰¤ W_k(N). If f: M â†’ N is a piecewise smooth map of nonzero degree between compact oriented n-manifolds, with the corresponding relative boundary condition in the case of manifolds with boundary, and if the k-dilation of f is at most Î», then Î» W_k(M) â‰¥ W_k(N).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the restriction monotonicity, take a family of flat k-cycles sweeping out N and restrict each integral Lipschitz chain to the open subset M. This restriction map commutes with boundary, is bounded for the flat norm, and sends the gluing homomorphism for N to the relative gluing homomorphism for M, so the restricted family sweeps out M and has no larger mass. For the map monotonicity, push forward a sweeping family F of k-cycles in M by f. Naturality of the gluing homomorphism implies that f(F) sweeps out N whenever f has nonzero degree. Since k-dilation at most Î» means that f sends every integral Lipschitz k-chain of mass m to a k-chain of mass at most Î»m, the width of f(F) is at most Î» times the width of F. Taking the infimum over F gives Î» W_k(M) â‰¥ W_k(N).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.1, Gromov non-degeneracy of width
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the standard round metric on S^n, the k-width W_k(S^n) is bounded below by a positive constant c(n) depending only on n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume there is a sweepout family F of k-cycles in S^n with arbitrarily small width. Approximate F by a finite complex of cycles C: to each i-simplex Î”^i in a fine triangulation of the parameter space, associate an integral Lipschitz (k+i)-chain C(Î”^i), with the boundary relation matching the simplicial boundary and with all higher-dimensional chains very small. The gluing homomorphism is nontrivial because F sweeps out S^n. If the vertex cycles have very small mass, the isoperimetric inequality fills each vertex cycle by a (k+1)-chain of small mass. Inductively, after filling all lower-dimensional simplices, each modified chain over an i-simplex becomes a small (k+i)-cycle, hence is null-homologous by the isoperimetric inequality, and can be filled with controlled mass. At the top dimension, a nontrivial glued n-cycle is expressed as a sum of tiny n-cycles, each null-homologous in S^n, contradicting nontriviality. Therefore the width cannot be arbitrarily small.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.2, k-width of a rectangle
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R = [0,R_1] Ã— ... Ã— [0,R_n] be an n-dimensional rectangle with side lengths ordered R_1 â‰¤ ... â‰¤ R_n. Then c(n) R_1...R_k â‰¤ W_k(R) â‰¤ R_1...R_k.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the upper bound, project R onto its last nâˆ’k coordinates. The fibers are k-rectangles with side lengths R_1,...,R_k, volume R_1...R_k, and they form a relative k-cycle sweepout of R. For the lower bound, map R linearly and degree one onto the unit n-cube by x_i â†¦ x_i/R_i. This map has k-dilation (R_1...R_k)^{-1}. By monotonicity of width under degree maps, (R_1...R_k)^{-1} W_k(R) â‰¥ W_k(unit cube). The unit cube contains a round ball or is controlled by the non-degeneracy result, so W_k(unit cube) â‰¥ c(n), giving W_k(R) â‰¥ c(n)R_1...R_k.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Construction 1 and Lemma 2.1, bending planes around a skeleton
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let B(R) be the radius-R ball in R^n and let S be the k-skeleton of a unit cubical lattice in R^n. There is a family F of k-cycles sweeping out B(R) such that each cycle in F is contained in S except for a subset of k-mass at most C(n), and the multiplicity with which any cycle uses S is bounded by C(n). The constant C(n) is independent of R. The construction uses the following PL mapping lemma: for each Îµ>0 there is a piecewise linear proper map Î¨: R^n â†’ R^n, linear on a triangulation whose top simplices are labeled good or bad, such that good simplices are mapped into S, bad simplices lie in the Îµ-neighborhood of the dual (nâˆ’kâˆ’1)-skeleton T, every unit ball meets at most C(n) simplices, |Î¨(x)âˆ’x|â‰¤C(n), and every simplex has diameter at most C(n).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose an (nâˆ’k)-plane P through the origin in general position with respect to the cubical skeleton, and begin with the family F_0 of all k-planes Q perpendicular to P. The desired family is Î¨(F_0). The map Î¨ is constructed using the dual skeleton T. For every k-face A of S and every dual (nâˆ’kâˆ’1)-face B of T in its link, take the convex hull K(A,B); these tiles cover R^n with disjoint interiors. Split K(A,B) into a good part away from B and a bad part lying within O(Îµ) of B, barycentrically subdivide all pieces, and define Î¨ on barycenters of faces so that good pieces collapse into A while bad pieces stretch across K(A,B). The link relation ensures consistency on common faces, and the tile geometry gives the uniform local-complexity, diameter, and displacement bounds independent of Îµ. Since Î¨ is bounded distance from the identity, the straight-line homotopy Î¨_t=(1âˆ’t)Î¨+t id is proper, so Î¨ has degree one. Good simplex contributions Î¨(Qâˆ©Î”) lie in S; bounded displacement and bounded local simplex count give bounded multiplicity on S. Bad simplices are controlled by transversality: inside a fixed large ball, the dual skeleton lies in finitely many (nâˆ’kâˆ’1)-planes T_i; for generic P, the projections of T_i to P meet transversely. Taking Îµ sufficiently small, a fiber Q of the projection to P meets the Îµ-neighborhood of only O(n) such T_i, hence only O(n) small balls and only C(n) bad simplices. The image of each bad piece has k-volume O(C(n)) because of the diameter and displacement bounds. Thus every Î¨(Q) has only C(n) mass off S and bounded multiplicity on S, and because Î¨ has degree one the family sweeps out B(R).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1, width-volume inequality
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let U be a bounded open subset of R^n with Euclidean volume V(U). Then its k-width satisfies W_k(U) < C(n) V(U)^{k/n}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By scaling, reduce to V(U)=1. Translate the unit cubical lattice and let S(x) be its k-skeleton. Averaging over x in a unit cube gives average k-volume |Uâˆ©S(x)| equal to a dimensional constant, so choose a translate S with |Uâˆ©S|â‰¤C(n). Put U inside a sufficiently large ball B(R) and apply Construction 1 to obtain a sweepout of B(R) by k-cycles each lying in S except for mass C(n), and with multiplicity on S bounded by C(n). Restrict this sweepout to U; restriction preserves the sweepout property for relative cycles. For each cycle E, decompose Eâˆ©U into the part in S and the part outside S. The in-skeleton mass is at most C(n)|Sâˆ©U|â‰¤C(n), and the off-skeleton mass is at most C(n). Hence every restricted cycle has k-mass at most C(n), proving W_k(U)â‰¤C(n) when V(U)=1; scaling gives the stated exponent k/n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.1, width-volume inequality for functions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f: R^n â†’ [0,1] be compactly supported. Define W_k(f) as the infimum, over k-cycle sweepouts of a large ball containing supp(f), of the supremum of the integrals of f over the cycles, with multiplicity. Then W_k(f) < C(n) (âˆ«_{R^n} f)^{k/n}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: After scaling assume âˆ«f=1. Translate the unit k-skeleton S so that âˆ«_S fâ‰¤C(n) by the same averaging argument used for sets. Apply the bending construction to sweep a large ball by cycles E that lie in S except for mass C(n), with bounded multiplicity on S. Split E into E_1âŠ‚S and the off-skeleton part E_2. The integral over E_1 is bounded by C(n)âˆ«_S fâ‰¤C(n); the integral over E_2 is bounded by mass(E_2) because 0â‰¤fâ‰¤1, hence also by C(n). Rescaling gives the general estimate.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.2, width-volume inequality for subsets of a rectangle
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R = [0,R_1] Ã— ... Ã— [0,R_n] be an n-rectangle with R_1â‰¤...â‰¤R_n, and let UâŠ‚R be open with volume V(U). For every integer l with 0â‰¤lâ‰¤k, one has W_k(U) < C(n) (R_1...R_l)^{(nâˆ’k)/(nâˆ’l)} V(U)^{(kâˆ’l)/(nâˆ’l)}, where the empty product for l=0 is 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The case l=0 is Theorem 1. The case l=k follows from monotonicity W_k(U)â‰¤W_k(R)â‰¤R_1...R_k. For 0<l<k, define a function f on the (nâˆ’l)-rectangle [0,R_{l+1}]Ã—...Ã—[0,R_n] by f(y)=(R_1...R_l)^{-1} Vol_l(Uâˆ©([0,R_1]Ã—...Ã—[0,R_l]Ã—{y})). Then 0â‰¤fâ‰¤1 and âˆ«f=(R_1...R_l)^{-1}V(U). By Proposition 2.1 in dimension nâˆ’l, f admits a sweepout by (kâˆ’l)-cycles F(p) for which âˆ«_{F(p)} fâ‰¤C(n)[(R_1...R_l)^{-1}V(U)]^{(kâˆ’l)/(nâˆ’l)}. Taking the product F(p)Ã—[0,R_1]Ã—...Ã—[0,R_l] gives k-cycles; restricting them to U yields a sweepout of U. The k-volume in U is at most (R_1...R_l) times the f-integral, giving exactly C(n)(R_1...R_l)^{(nâˆ’k)/(nâˆ’l)}V(U)^{(kâˆ’l)/(nâˆ’l)}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.3, packing-width of a rectangle
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a bounded open set U, define P_{k,N}(U) to be the supremal W such that U contains N disjoint subsets U_i each satisfying W_k(U_i)â‰¥W. If R is an n-rectangle with side lengths R_1â‰¤...â‰¤R_n, then P_{k,N}(R), up to multiplicative constants C(n), equals the infimum over integers 0â‰¤lâ‰¤k of R_1...R_l (R_{l+1}...R_n)^{(kâˆ’l)/(nâˆ’l)} N^{âˆ’(kâˆ’l)/(nâˆ’l)}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the upper bound, take any N disjoint subsets U_iâŠ‚R. One has volume at most Vol(R)/N. Applying Proposition 2.2 to that subset, for each l, gives W_k(U_i)â‰¤C(n)(R_1...R_l)^{(nâˆ’k)/(nâˆ’l)}(Vol(R)/N)^{(kâˆ’l)/(nâˆ’l)}, which simplifies to C(n)R_1...R_l(R_{l+1}...R_n)^{(kâˆ’l)/(nâˆ’l)}N^{âˆ’(kâˆ’l)/(nâˆ’l)}. Therefore P_{k,N}(R) is bounded above by the infimum of these expressions. For the lower bound, use rectangular grid packings. If N is small enough, specifically Nâ‰¤(R_{k+1}...R_n)/R_k^{nâˆ’k}, one can pack N subrectangles of dimensions R_1Ã—...Ã—R_kÃ—R_kÃ—...Ã—R_k, each having k-width comparable to R_1...R_k. If N is larger, choose l<k and S with R_lâ‰¤Sâ‰¤R_{l+1} so that N subrectangles of dimensions R_1Ã—...Ã—R_lÃ—SÃ—...Ã—S fill a definite fraction of R; then N R_1...R_l S^{nâˆ’l}â‰¥c(n)Vol(R), and each such subrectangle has k-width comparable to R_1...R_l S^{kâˆ’l}. Solving for S yields the matching lower expression. These grid packings match one of the upper bounds up to C(n).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.1, derivative formula for k-dilation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a piecewise smooth map f between n-dimensional Riemannian manifolds, the k-dilation of f equals sup_x ||Î›^k df_x||, the supremal operator norm of the induced linear map on k-vectors. If the singular values of df_x are 0â‰¤s_1â‰¤...â‰¤s_n, then ||Î›^k df_x||=s_{nâˆ’k+1}...s_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The singular k-vectors for Î›^k df_x are wedge products of k singular vectors of df_x, and the corresponding singular values are all products of k distinct singular values of df_x. The largest product is s_{nâˆ’k+1}...s_n and is achieved on a simple k-vector, so a small k-disk tangent to the corresponding singular directions is stretched by this factor to first order. Conversely every infinitesimal k-disk has volume stretch at most this operator norm. A standard calculus/localization argument gives equality between the global k-dilation and the supremum of these pointwise norms.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.2, higher dilations controlled by k-dilation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f be a piecewise smooth map between n-dimensional Riemannian manifolds. If the k-dilation of f is at most Î», then for every i>0 with k+iâ‰¤n, the (k+i)-dilation of f is at most Î»^{(k+i)/k}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: At a point, write the singular values of df as s_1â‰¤...â‰¤s_n. The hypothesis gives s_{nâˆ’k+1}...s_nâ‰¤Î», so in particular s_{nâˆ’k+1}â‰¤Î»^{1/k}. The (k+i)-dilation density is s_{nâˆ’kâˆ’i+1}...s_n. Since the i extra factors are no larger than s_{nâˆ’k+1}, this product is at most s_{nâˆ’k+1}^i(s_{nâˆ’k+1}...s_n)â‰¤Î»^{i/k}Î»=Î»^{(k+i)/k}. Taking the supremum over points proves the result.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.3, basic lower bound for D_k between rectangles
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R and S be n-rectangles with ordered side lengths R_1â‰¤...â‰¤R_n and S_1â‰¤...â‰¤S_n, and put Q_i=S_i/R_i. If UâŠ‚R and f:(U,âˆ‚U)â†’(S,âˆ‚S) is a nonzero-degree piecewise smooth map with k-dilation Î», then Î»â‰¥c(n) Q_1...Q_k. Equivalently, the infimal k-dilation D_k(U,S) is at least c(n)Q_1...Q_k.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By monotonicity under subsets, W_k(U)â‰¤W_k(R)â‰¤R_1...R_k. Since f has nonzero degree and k-dilation Î», width monotonicity under degree maps gives Î» W_k(U)â‰¥W_k(S). Proposition 1.2 gives W_k(S)â‰¥c(n)S_1...S_k. Combining these inequalities gives Î»â‰¥c(n)S_1...S_k/(R_1...R_k)=c(n)Q_1...Q_k.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.4, packing-width lower bounds for D_k between rectangles
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R and S be n-rectangles with ordered side lengths R_1â‰¤...â‰¤R_n and S_1â‰¤...â‰¤S_n, and let Q_i=S_i/R_i. If UâŠ‚R, then for every integer l with 0â‰¤lâ‰¤k, the infimal k-dilation D_k(U,S) of a nonzero-degree map from U to S satisfies D_k(U,S) â‰¥ c(n) Q_1...Q_l (Q_{l+1}...Q_n)^{(kâˆ’l)/(nâˆ’l)}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let f:Uâ†’S have nonzero degree and k-dilation Î». For any N, take N disjoint subsets S_iâŠ‚S with k-width nearly P_{k,N}(S). Their preimages U_i=f^{-1}(S_i) are disjoint subsets of U, and the restricted maps have nonzero degree in the relative sense. Width monotonicity gives W_k(U_i)â‰¥Î»^{-1}W_k(S_i), so Î» P_{k,N}(U)â‰¥P_{k,N}(S). Since UâŠ‚R, P_{k,N}(U)â‰¤P_{k,N}(R), hence Î»â‰¥P_{k,N}(S)/P_{k,N}(R). Now choose N so that S contains a grid of roughly N subrectangles with dimensions S_1Ã—...Ã—S_lÃ—S_lÃ—...Ã—S_l when lâ‰¥1, or cubes of side S_1 when l=0; each has k-width comparable to S_1...S_l S_l^{kâˆ’l} or S_1^k in the l=0 case. Apply the upper estimate for P_{k,N}(R) from Proposition 2.3 with the same l. After substituting N and simplifying, the quotient is bounded below by c(n)Q_1...Q_l(Q_{l+1}...Q_n)^{(kâˆ’l)/(nâˆ’l)}. The paper notes that optimizing over N gives no further lower bounds beyond the maximum of these monomials, up to constants.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Boundary-volume lower bound for (nâˆ’1)-dilation in the proof of Theorem 2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R and S be n-rectangles with ordered side lengths R_1â‰¤...â‰¤R_n and S_1â‰¤...â‰¤S_n, and put Q_i=S_i/R_i. If f:(R,âˆ‚R)â†’(S,âˆ‚S) is degree one with (nâˆ’1)-dilation Î», then Î»â‰¥(1/n) Q_2...Q_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The restriction f|_{âˆ‚R}:âˆ‚Râ†’âˆ‚S must be surjective because f has degree one as a map of pairs. Therefore Î» Vol_{nâˆ’1}(âˆ‚R)â‰¥Vol_{nâˆ’1}(âˆ‚S). Since R_1â‰¤...â‰¤R_n, each boundary face of R has (nâˆ’1)-volume at most R_2...R_n, so Vol(âˆ‚R)â‰¤2n R_2...R_n. The two faces of S perpendicular to the first coordinate have total volume 2S_2...S_n, so Vol(âˆ‚S)â‰¥2S_2...S_n. Combining gives Î»â‰¥S_2...S_n/(nR_2...R_n)=(1/n)Q_2...Q_n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Construction 2, the snake map
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let nâ‰¥3 and 2â‰¤kâ‰¤nâˆ’1. Let R and S be n-rectangles with side lengths ordered as usual. Suppose R_i=S_i for all iâ‰¤nâˆ’k, and suppose R_{nâˆ’k+1}...R_{nâˆ’k+b} â‰¥ S_{nâˆ’k+1}...S_{nâˆ’k+b} for every b=1,...,k. Then there is a degree one map f:(R,âˆ‚R)â†’(S,âˆ‚S) with k-dilation at most C(n).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write R=R'Ã—R'' and S=S'Ã—S'', where R' and S' have dimension nâˆ’k and are congruent, and R'',S'' have dimension k. The product inequalities imply that a dilated copy 3S'' admits a smooth C(n)-bilipschitz embedding I into R''; call its image A. Choose a smooth cutoff H on 3S'' equal to 1 on the central S'' and zero near the boundary, with Lipschitz constant approximately S_{nâˆ’k+1}^{âˆ’1}; extend Hâˆ˜I^{-1} by zero to all of R'', calling the extension Hbar. Define Î¦_1(x',x'')=(Hbar(x'')x',x''). Because R_{nâˆ’k}=S_{nâˆ’k}â‰¤S_{nâˆ’k+1}, the Lipschitz constant of Î¦_1 is bounded by C(n), and its image lies in Q=(R'Ã—A)âˆª({0}Ã—R''). Next choose a piecewise smooth retraction Ï†_2:R''â†’A mapping R''\A to âˆ‚A, and define Î¦_2(x',x'')=(x',Ï†_2(x'')). Although Î¦_2 may have large k-dilation on all of R, its restriction to Q has k-dilation at most 1: it is the identity on R'Ã—A, while on {0}Ã—(R''\A) it maps a k-dimensional set into {0}Ã—âˆ‚A, of dimension kâˆ’1. Then define Î¦_3(x',x'')=(x',I^{-1}(x'')), a C(n)-bilipschitz map from R'Ã—A to S'Ã—3S''. Finally retract S'Ã—3S'' onto S by a 1-Lipschitz convex retraction Î¦_4. The composition Î¦_4âˆ˜Î¦_3âˆ˜Î¦_2âˆ˜Î¦_1 is degree one and has k-dilation bounded by C(n).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2, optimal (nâˆ’1)-dilation between rectangles up to constants
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R and S be n-dimensional rectangles with ordered side lengths R_1â‰¤...â‰¤R_n and S_1â‰¤...â‰¤S_n, and define Q_i=S_i/R_i. Let D_{nâˆ’1}(R,S) be the infimal (nâˆ’1)-dilation of a degree one map (R,âˆ‚R)â†’(S,âˆ‚S). Up to a multiplicative constant C(n), D_{nâˆ’1}(R,S) is the maximum of the following n monomials: for each l=1,...,nâˆ’1, Q_1...Q_l (Q_{l+1}...Q_n)^{(nâˆ’lâˆ’1)/(nâˆ’l)}, and the additional monomial Q_2...Q_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The lower bounds are assembled from earlier estimates. The monomials with l=1,...,nâˆ’1 are exactly the Proposition 3.4 packing-width lower bounds with k=nâˆ’1. The final monomial Q_2...Q_n is the boundary-volume lower bound: a degree one map sends âˆ‚R onto âˆ‚S, so Î»Vol(âˆ‚R)â‰¥Vol(âˆ‚S), and the ordered side lengths imply Vol(âˆ‚R)â‰¤2nR_2...R_n and Vol(âˆ‚S)â‰¥2S_2...S_n. For upper bounds, the proof rescales S so that the stated lower-bound maximum is 1. This gives inequalities (*) consisting of R_1...R_l(R_{l+1}...R_n)^{(nâˆ’lâˆ’1)/(nâˆ’l)} â‰¥ S_1...S_l(S_{l+1}...S_n)^{(nâˆ’lâˆ’1)/(nâˆ’l)} for l=1,...,nâˆ’1, and R_2...R_nâ‰¥S_2...S_n. In dimension n=3, the proof handles three cases: if R_1<S_1, first use a 2-contracting linear map to an intermediate T with T_1=S_1 and then a snake map Tâ†’S; if R_1â‰¥S_1 but R_2<S_2, use a 2-contracting linear map to T with T_2=S_2 and then a 1-contracting linear map Tâ†’S; if R_1â‰¥S_1 and R_2â‰¥S_2, use the snake map directly. For higher n, the main lemma treats the case R_1=S_1. It introduces conditions C(p): R_1=S_1, product inequalities R_2...R_aâ‰¥S_2...S_a for 2â‰¤aâ‰¤p, the mixed inequalities R_2...R_l(R_{l+1}...R_n)^{(nâˆ’lâˆ’1)/(nâˆ’l)}â‰¥S_2...S_l(S_{l+1}...S_n)^{(nâˆ’lâˆ’1)/(nâˆ’l)} for pâ‰¤lâ‰¤nâˆ’1, and R_2...R_nâ‰¥S_2...S_n. The proof descends in p. Condition C(nâˆ’1) gives the hypotheses of the snake map. If C(p) holds but a first product R_2...R_b<S_2...S_b occurs, with p<bâ‰¤nâˆ’2, the proof applies a sequence of (nâˆ’1)-expanding linear transformations to S, decreasing a block of side lengths around b and increasing later side lengths equally, until an intermediate T satisfies R_2...R_b=T_2...T_b while preserving the relevant mixed quantities. Then R and T satisfy C(b), so the induction hypothesis gives a good map Râ†’T, and the linear map Tâ†’S is (nâˆ’1)-contracting. The cases R_1<S_1 and R_1>S_1 are reduced to the equal-first-side lemma by further (nâˆ’1)-contracting or (nâˆ’1)-expanding linear transformations of S to an intermediate T with T_1=R_1, checking algebraically that the inequalities (*) are preserved. Composing the resulting maps gives degree one maps with (nâˆ’1)-dilation bounded by C(n), matching the lower bounds.

proof_techniques.name: Sweepouts plus gluing homomorphism
proof_techniques.description: The paper encodes a sweepout as a continuous family of flat k-cycles whose associated gluing homomorphism from the homology of the parameter space to H_n(M,âˆ‚M;Z) is nontrivial. A fine triangulation of the parameter space and small flat-norm approximations turn such a family into a chain-level complex C assigning (k+i)-chains to i-simplices, with boundaries matching simplicial boundaries. This model makes it possible to prove naturality under maps and restriction to subdomains.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a degree one map f:(R,âˆ‚R)â†’(S,âˆ‚S), pushforward of sweeping cycles or pullback of wide target subsets can convert the degree hypothesis and Dil_2(f)â‰¤1 into lower bounds on the widths of subsets of R.

proof_techniques.name: Bending planes around a cubical skeleton
proof_techniques.description: To prove width-volume estimates, Guth begins with parallel k-planes and composes them with a proper degree-one PL map Î¨ that is bounded distance from the identity. The map collapses most of space into a fixed translated cubical k-skeleton S, while only a small neighborhood of the dual (nâˆ’kâˆ’1)-skeleton can contribute off-skeleton mass. A generic choice of plane directions ensures each bent plane meets only boundedly many bad regions, while averaging over translations ensures U has small intersection with S.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is the engine behind estimates such as W_2(U)â‰¤C Vol(U)^{1/2} and the sharper rectangle-relative estimates for preimage sets UâŠ‚R. It is especially relevant if one subdivides S and studies disjoint preimages in R.

proof_techniques.name: Rectangle-adapted width-volume via slicing off short directions
proof_techniques.description: For UâŠ‚R, the paper improves the raw Euclidean estimate by slicing U along the first l short directions of R. The normalized fiber-volume function f on the remaining nâˆ’l coordinates satisfies 0â‰¤fâ‰¤1 and âˆ«f=Vol(U)/(R_1...R_l). A width-volume estimate for f, then taking products with the l-dimensional short rectangle, gives W_k(U)â‰¤C(R_1...R_l)^{(nâˆ’k)/(nâˆ’l)}Vol(U)^{(kâˆ’l)/(nâˆ’l)}.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In dimension 4 with k=2, the l=1 estimate bounds the 2-width of a preimage subset using R_1 and its volume; the hypothesis that R_1 is small relative to S_1 is exactly the kind of information this refinement exploits.

proof_techniques.name: Packing-width lower bounds by disjoint target blocks
proof_techniques.description: The paper defines P_{k,N}(U) as the largest width W for which U contains N disjoint subsets of k-width at least W. If a degree map f:Uâ†’S has k-dilation Î», then preimages of N disjoint wide subsets in S are N disjoint subsets of U with width reduced by at most Î», so Î»P_{k,N}(U)â‰¥P_{k,N}(S). Rectangular grid packings give sharp estimates of P_{k,N}(R) up to C(n), leading to explicit monomial lower bounds for D_k(R,S).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A natural route is to choose many disjoint subrectangles of the target S, estimate their 2-width from below, and use the rectangle-adapted upper bounds for the 2-width of their disjoint preimages in R.

proof_techniques.name: Singular-value bootstrapping from k-dilation to higher dilation
proof_techniques.description: Using the pointwise singular values of df, Guth proves that if Dil_k(f)â‰¤Î» then Dil_{k+i}(f)â‰¤Î»^{(k+i)/k}. The argument is purely linear algebraic and depends on the fact that the largest (k+i)-fold product of singular values is controlled by the largest k-fold product times i additional factors, each at most Î»^{1/k}.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: With Dil_2(f)â‰¤1 in four dimensions, one immediately gets Dil_3(f)â‰¤1 and Dil_4(f)â‰¤1. This allows use of the paper's (nâˆ’1)-dilation estimates and boundary-volume obstruction in addition to the direct 2-width estimates.

proof_techniques.name: Boundary-volume obstruction for hypersurface dilation
proof_techniques.description: For maps between rectangles, a degree one map of pairs restricts to a surjective map of boundaries. If its (nâˆ’1)-dilation is Î», the boundary volume of the target is at most Î» times the boundary volume of the source. Ordered side lengths turn this into Î»â‰¥c(n)S_2...S_n/(R_2...R_n).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: After applying singular-value bootstrapping, Dil_2(f)â‰¤1 gives Dil_3(f)â‰¤1 in dimension 4, hence a necessary inequality involving R_2R_3R_4 and S_2S_3S_4.

proof_techniques.name: Scaling normalization of homogeneous inequalities
proof_techniques.description: The estimates for D_k(R,S) are homogeneous in the target side lengths. In proving Theorem 2, Guth rescales S so that the maximum lower-bound monomial is normalized to 1, then proves that the resulting inequalities imply existence of a map with bounded dilation. This reduces algebraic comparisons to dimensionless side-length product inequalities.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The alternatives in the original problem are homogeneous side-length inequalities up to an absolute constant. A contradiction proof can similarly normalize one target or source scale and track which monomial lower bounds become larger than 1.

proof_techniques.name: Snake-map factorization
proof_techniques.description: The snake map constructs low k-dilation degree-one maps by first embedding a dilated copy of the target's last k-dimensional rectangle into the source's last k-dimensional rectangle as a winding set A, then collapsing the source to a union R'Ã—A âˆª {0}Ã—R'', retracting this union to R'Ã—A, undoing the bilipschitz embedding, and finally retracting to the target. The key point is that the potentially high-Lipschitz retraction has zero k-dimensional Jacobian on the collapsed part and is the identity on the main part.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Although the original problem asks for a lower bound, the snake construction explains why linear stretching estimates alone are insufficient; lower-bound arguments need robust sweepout or packing methods that survive such nonlinear collapses.

other_useful_info: All constants c(n) and C(n) in the paper depend only on dimension and may change from line to line. The paper explicitly states as an open problem the estimation of D_k(R,S) up to a constant factor for 2â‰¤kâ‰¤nâˆ’2; thus the case n=4,k=2 lies in the intermediate range for which this paper gives general lower bounds but not a complete sharp formula. The paper cites Guth's thesis [9] for additional upper and lower bounds for intermediate k-dilation between rectangles. Proposition 4.1 shows that for every nâ‰¥3 and 2â‰¤kâ‰¤nâˆ’1 there are rectangles R,S for which the best linear k-dilation is larger than the optimal nonlinear k-dilation by an arbitrarily large factor; the example uses R with dimensions Îµ,...,Îµ,1,1 and S with dimensions Îµ,...,Îµ,Îµ^{âˆ’1}, together with the snake map. The appendix also records Falconer's theorem that for k>n/2, bounded open UâŠ‚R^n has small linear k-width controlled by Vol(U)^{k/n}, and cites Bourgain for a weaker linear 2-width estimate in R^4; these are related to but separate from the nonlinear width machinery used in the main lower bounds.
why_relevant: This paper proves lower bounds for k-dilation of degree-one maps between Euclidean domains and estimates such bounds for rectangles. Its width/sweepout machinery is directly relevant to turning Dil_2(f) â‰¤ 1 and degree one into side-length inequalities.
